_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2 + LA trim + apps + extra exercises + exercises unify v2 + second figure
title: Credit cards: the minimum-payment trap
deck: Revolving credit, no fixed term. The monthly arithmetic is short, but minimum payments at credit-card rates can stretch a moderate balance into a decade-long debt.
objectives: Compute the unpaid balance after a credit card payment and identify why minimum payments dramatically extend the time and total cost of paying off a balance.

content.text: Imagine carrying a $5,000 balance on a credit card at 24% APR, and paying $100 toward it every month. A quick guess would say the balance is dropping by $100 per statement and will be paid off in about fifty months. The actual outcome is much worse than that. After the first month's interest is added, the $100 payment exactly cancels the interest charge, the balance stays at $5,000, and the slow drain never even begins.

Credit cards differ from auto loans in two important ways. First, there is no fixed term: the cardholder decides how much to pay each month, and the bank only requires a small minimum. Second, the amortization formula from Section 4.2 does not apply directly, because there is no agreement to drain the balance to zero by a specific date. Instead, the monthly math is a short two-line update, and this section walks through that update and shows why minimum-only payments are one of the most expensive financial decisions in the consumer credit world.

Every credit-card statement updates the balance using a simple two-step rule. First, the bank charges interest on the balance from the previous statement: interest = balance × r/12. Second, the cardholder's payment is subtracted, and any new purchases since the last statement are added. The result is the new balance carried into next month, and the cycle repeats.

content.caption: Figure 4.4. One statement on a $5,000 balance at 24% APR. The $200 payment splits exactly in half: $100 covers the new interest and only $100 pays down principal. The balance drops to $4,900, not $4,800.

content.caption: Three payment strategies on the same $5,000 balance at 22% APR. Minimum-only payments stretch the debt past 30 years and pay more in interest than the original balance. Doubling the minimum cuts the timeline to three years and the interest to a few hundred dollars.

content.text:
Definition 4.4. For a credit-card statement period with starting balance B, annual rate r, and payment M (with no new purchases),
interest = B · [r] / [12]
new balance = B + interest − M
The minimum payment is the smallest payment the bank will accept on a statement, typically 1–3% of the current balance.

Margin noteMost credit-card agreements compute interest on the average daily balance rather than the statement-closing balance, and they compound daily, which makes the effective monthly interest charge slightly higher than the headline r/12. For introductory work we will keep using r/12; the trade-off is a small overestimate of the principal paid down each month.

A minimum payment is the smallest amount the cardholder can pay without triggering late fees or a delinquency notice. For most cards, the minimum is set to roughly the previous month's interest plus 1% of the principal. Paying only the minimum keeps the account in good standing, but the principal barely moves, the next statement's interest charge is barely smaller, and the cycle can stretch into years.
A minimum payment buys time. It does not buy progress.

Common mistakeTreating the minimum payment as a recommended payment. The minimum is designed to keep the account current; it is not designed to pay off the balance in any reasonable amount of time. A $5,000 balance at 24% APR, paid only the minimum, can take more than twenty years to retire, with thousands of dollars of additional interest along the way.

Example 4.4. $5,000 at 24% APR, $200 a month.

A cardholder owes $5,000 on a credit card with a 24% APR. The minimum payment is around $130 a month, but the cardholder commits to a flat $200 per month and stops using the card for new purchases. We want the new balance after the first statement, and a quick sense of how long the full balance will take to pay off.

Solution.

Step 1. Compute this month's interest. The monthly rate is r/12 = 0.24/12 = 0.02, so:

interest = 5,000 × 0.02 = $100

Step 2. Update the balance. Add the interest, subtract the payment:

new balance = 5,000 + 100 − 200 = $4,900

Step 3. Interpret the split. The cardholder paid $200, but only $100 went to principal; the other $100 was rent on the borrowed money. Exactly half of the payment did no work to reduce the debt.

Step 4. Estimate the payoff horizon. If the cardholder keeps paying $200 per month, the time to fully retire the balance turns out to be about thirty-two months, with total interest paid of roughly $1,375. Paying only the (declining) minimum payment, on the other hand, takes more than twenty years and produces close to $13,000 of total interest. The same $5,000 debt becomes a totally different cost depending only on the chosen monthly payment.

The lesson is not arithmetic. It is that the gap between paying a comfortable round number every month and paying the minimum is the difference between a few years of debt and a generation of it. The numbers can be checked using the Excel function =NPER(rate, −payment, balance), which returns the number of months to payoff at any fixed payment.

In the wildThe Federal Reserve's Survey of Consumer Finances estimates that the median US household carries a few thousand dollars of revolving credit-card debt. At the average new-card APR of about 22%, paying only the minimum on that median balance translates to decades of interest and one of the highest effective borrowing rates the average consumer ever encounters. The arithmetic in this section is, in the most literal sense, a financial-literacy intervention.

content.problem: A credit card has a balance of $3,000 and an APR of 18%. What is this month's interest charge?
Using the same balance and APR, suppose the cardholder pays $150 this month and makes no new purchases. What is the new balance after the payment?
For a $5,000 balance at 24% APR with a $100 monthly payment and no new purchases, what is the new balance after one statement period?
content.answer: $5,000. The interest is 5,000 × 0.02 = $100, and the new balance is 5,000 + 100 − 100 = 5,000. The payment exactly cancels the interest; the principal does not move at all.

content.problem:
Why does paying only the minimum on a high-APR credit card take so long to retire a balance?

  1. The minimum payment is computed to barely exceed the interest charge.
  2. The bank charges extra fees.
  3. The principal increases each month.
  4. The APR rises automatically.
content.answer: A. Minimum payments are designed to keep the account current; very little of each payment reduces principal.

content.problem:
Which Excel function computes the number of months to fully repay a credit-card balance at a fixed monthly payment?

  1. =PMT()
  2. =PV()
  3. =NPER()
  4. =FV()
content.answer: C. NPER() returns the number of periods required, given the rate, payment, and present-value balance.

content.problem:
A $4,000 balance at 21% APR is paid down with $250 per month. What is the new balance after one statement?

  1. $3,750
  2. $3,820
  3. $3,930
  4. $4,070
content.answer: B ($3,820). The monthly rate is 0.21/12 = 0.0175, the interest is 4,000 × 0.0175 = $70, and the new balance is 4,000 + 70 − 250 = $3,820. Option A is the trap of ignoring the interest charge; option D would be the balance after no payment at all.

content.problem: Your credit card has a **$4,000 balance** at **19.99% APR**. The minimum payment is $80. After one month: (a) How much interest accrued? (b) What is the new balance?
content.answer: (a) Interest = 4,000 × (0.1999/12) ≈ 4,000 × 0.01666 = **$66.63**. (b) New balance = 4,000 + 66.63 − 80 = **$3,986.63**. Only $13.37 went to principal — the rest went to interest.

content.problem: Suppose the same card user pays **$200/month** instead of the $80 minimum. Roughly how much of the **$200** goes to principal in the first month? (Hint: interest stays at ~$66.63.)

content.title: One credit-card statement
content.problem: Your credit card balance is **$5,000** at the start of the month. The card's APR is **18%**. You pay the **minimum payment of $100**. What is the balance at the end of the month?
content.solution:
**Apply the credit-card balance formula.**

Interest accrued this month: balance × (r/12) = 5,000 × (0.18/12) = 5,000 × 0.015 = **$75**.

New balance: old balance + interest − payment = 5,000 + 75 − 100 = **$4,975**.

After a month, you only reduced principal by **$25** (your $100 payment had to cover $75 of interest first). At this rate, paying off the $5,000 would take **decades**.

content.title: The minimum-payment trap
content.problem: Suppose you have a **$5,000** credit-card balance at **18% APR**, and you pay **only the 2% minimum payment** each month (2% of the *current* balance). How long until the balance is paid off?
content.solution:
**Set up the per-month math.**

Each month, interest is 0.18/12 = 1.5% of the balance. The minimum payment is 2% of the balance. So the *net reduction* is only **0.5%** of the balance per month.

At that rate, a $5,000 balance shrinks by only **$25** in the first month — and that fraction shrinks each subsequent month as the balance shrinks. Solving the geometric series gives a payoff time on the order of **decades** with thousands paid in interest, even though the original balance was $5,000.

*The takeaway.* Minimum payments are not designed to retire the debt — they are designed to keep you in good standing while the lender continues earning interest. Paying anything above the minimum changes the math dramatically.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Credit cards use the same monthly-interest math as a mortgage (interest = balance × r/12), but applied to a *revolving* balance with no fixed term. Minimum payments often barely cover the interest, which is why even modest balances at card-typical APRs of 18-26% can persist for decades. Paying above the minimum is the only way to make progress.

content.title: The minimum-payment trap
content.content: Average US household credit card debt: **about $7,000** (Federal Reserve, 2024). Average credit card APR: **about 22%**. If a household with $7,000 in debt makes only the minimum payment (typically 2-3% of balance, often $25-40), the math compounds against them: interest accrues faster than principal pays down. The minimum-payment trap is not metaphorical — it's an arithmetic outcome of monthly compounding at high rates with insufficient payments. Paying $50 above minimum can shave years off the payoff date and thousands off the total interest. The escape route is in the math.

content.text: Credit cards reveal what happens when the borrower controls the payment.
